course_info.title: Introduction to Linguistics
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 3 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: What Is Linguistics? The Scientific Study of Language
learning_objectives: Define linguistics as the scientific study of language structure and use
Distinguish between descriptive and prescriptive approaches to language
Identify the major subfields of linguistics: phonetics, phonology, morphology, syntax, and semantics
readings: Fromkin et al., An Introduction to Language, Ch. 1
Pullum, 'African American Vernacular English Is Not Standard English with Mistakes' (essay)

lecture_notes.introduction: Linguistics is not about correct grammar. It is the scientific study of how language actually works — its sounds, structures, meanings, and social functions. This course provides the foundational tools for analyzing language, which is essential for anyone who studies literature.
key_concepts: Descriptive vs. prescriptive linguistics: studying how people actually speak vs. telling them how they should speak
Language as a rule-governed system: all languages and dialects have consistent, complex grammatical rules
The arbitrariness of the sign: there is no natural connection between a word and what it represents
Language universals: features shared by all human languages despite surface differences
The subfields: phonetics (sounds), phonology (sound systems), morphology (word structure), syntax (sentence structure), semantics (meaning), pragmatics (language in context)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a sentence in African American Vernacular English to demonstrate that it follows consistent grammatical rules (habitual 'be,' negative concord, remote past 'been') that differ from but are equally systematic as Standard English rules

review.value: The study of how language actually works in practice, without judgment about correctness — every dialect, register, and variety has its own systematic grammar, and no variety is linguistically superior to another

Saussure's foundational principle: the relationship between a word (signifier) and its meaning (signified) is arbitrary — there is nothing inherently 'doglike' about the sounds d-o-g, which is why different languages use different words for the same concept

class_schedule.topic: Phonetics and Phonology: The Sounds of Language
learning_objectives: Transcribe English words using the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)
Classify consonants by place of articulation, manner of articulation, and voicing
Distinguish between phonetics (physical sounds) and phonology (sound systems in specific languages)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every language selects from the vast inventory of sounds the human vocal tract can produce. Phonetics studies those sounds physically; phonology studies how specific languages organize them into meaningful systems. The difference between the two is the difference between the physics of sound and the linguistics of meaning.
key_concepts: The International Phonetic Alphabet: a universal system for transcribing speech sounds
Consonant classification: place (bilabial, alveolar, velar...), manner (stop, fricative, nasal...), voicing (voiced vs. voiceless)
Vowel classification: tongue height, tongue position (front/back), and lip rounding
Phonemes vs. allophones: meaningful sound distinctions vs. predictable variants
Minimal pairs: pairs of words that differ by one sound (bat/pat), proving that the sound difference is phonemic
lecture_notes.demonstration: Practicing IPA transcription of everyday English words, then transcribing a word from a non-English language to demonstrate that the IPA works across all languages

review.value: A phoneme is a sound that distinguishes meaning (/p/ and /b/ are different phonemes because 'pat' and 'bat' mean different things). An allophone is a predictable variant that does not distinguish meaning (the aspirated [ph] in 'pat' and the unaspirated [p] in 'spat' are allophones of the same phoneme /p/)
Two words that are identical except for one sound (e.g., 'sip' and 'zip') — if the difference in sound produces a difference in meaning, the two sounds are separate phonemes in that language

learning_objectives: Identify morphemes — the smallest meaningful units of language — in complex words
Distinguish between free morphemes and bound morphemes, derivational and inflectional affixes
Analyze word formation processes: affixation, compounding, blending, clipping, and conversion

lecture_notes.introduction: Words are not atoms — they are molecules built from smaller meaningful parts. Morphology is the study of how words are constructed from morphemes, and understanding morphology reveals the systematic creativity of language.
key_concepts: Morphemes: the smallest units of language that carry meaning — 'unhappiness' has three: un-, happy, -ness
Free vs. bound morphemes: 'happy' can stand alone; '-ness' and 'un-' cannot
Derivational vs. inflectional morphology: creating new words (teach → teacher) vs. marking grammatical relations (teach → teaches)
Word formation processes: affixation, compounding (blackbird), blending (brunch), clipping (lab from laboratory), conversion (to google)
Morphological analysis: breaking words into their component morphemes and identifying each one's function
lecture_notes.demonstration: Morphological analysis exercise: breaking down a set of unfamiliar words into their component morphemes and identifying the function of each — then doing the same with words from a non-English language to demonstrate that morphological analysis works universally

review.value: The smallest meaningful unit of language. 'Unbreakable' contains three morphemes: un- (negative prefix), break (root meaning 'to fracture'), -able (suffix meaning 'capable of being'). A word can be a single morpheme ('cat') or many ('antidisestablishmentarianism' has approximately seven)

Derivational morphology creates new words, often changing the part of speech: 'happy' (adjective) → 'happiness' (noun). Inflectional morphology marks grammatical relationships without changing the part of speech: 'walk' → 'walks' (third person singular present tense)

learning_objectives: Analyze sentence structure using phrase structure trees
Identify the basic phrase types: noun phrase (NP), verb phrase (VP), prepositional phrase (PP)
Understand structural ambiguity: sentences with two possible syntactic analyses and therefore two meanings

lecture_notes.introduction: Syntax is the system of rules by which words combine into phrases and phrases combine into sentences. Every speaker of a language knows these rules unconsciously — they can produce and understand sentences they have never encountered before. Syntax makes this infinite creativity possible.
key_concepts: Phrase structure: sentences are not strings of words but hierarchically organized structures of phrases
Phrase structure rules: NP → Det N, VP → V NP, S → NP VP — the patterns that generate grammatical sentences
Tree diagrams: visual representations of hierarchical sentence structure
Structural ambiguity: 'I saw the man with the telescope' — who has the telescope?
Recursion: the ability to embed phrases within phrases without limit, generating infinitely many sentences from finite rules
lecture_notes.demonstration: Drawing phrase structure trees for sentences of increasing complexity, then encountering a structurally ambiguous sentence and drawing both possible trees to demonstrate that the ambiguity is structural, not lexical

review.value: A sentence like 'The teacher said the student failed on Monday' has two possible structures: the failure happened on Monday, or the saying happened on Monday. The ambiguity exists because the prepositional phrase 'on Monday' can attach to either verb phrase — two different trees, two different meanings

The property of syntax that allows phrases to be embedded within phrases of the same type: 'The cat that the dog that the boy owned chased ran away' — recursion is what makes human language infinite in expressive power

class_schedule.topic: Semantics and Pragmatics: Meaning in Language
learning_objectives: Distinguish between semantic meaning (what words and sentences mean) and pragmatic meaning (what speakers mean)
Analyze sense relations: synonymy, antonymy, hyponymy, and polysemy
Understand Grice's cooperative principle and conversational implicature

lecture_notes.introduction: Meaning is the whole point of language — but meaning turns out to be far more complicated than it appears. Semantics studies what words and sentences mean; pragmatics studies what speakers mean by using them. The difference is enormous and endlessly interesting.
key_concepts: Semantics: the study of meaning in language — word meaning, sentence meaning, and truth conditions
Sense relations: how words relate to each other — synonyms, antonyms, hyponyms (dog is a hyponym of animal)
Compositional semantics: how the meaning of a sentence is built from the meanings of its parts and their structure
Pragmatics: meaning in context — what speakers intend beyond what they literally say
Grice's cooperative principle: speakers cooperate by being informative, truthful, relevant, and clear — and violations of these maxims create implicature
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a conversation where a speaker violates Grice's maxims to create an implicature: 'Can you pass the salt?' is literally a yes/no question about ability, but pragmatically it is a request — the hearer understands the intended meaning through the cooperative principle

review.value: Meaning that is communicated indirectly through violation of conversational expectations. When asked 'How was the concert?' and replying 'The parking lot was nice,' the speaker implicates that the concert was terrible — by violating the maxim of relevance, they communicate more than they literally say

The principle that the meaning of a sentence is determined by the meanings of its words and the way they are combined — 'the dog bit the man' and 'the man bit the dog' have the same words but different meanings because of their different syntactic structures

class_schedule.topic: Language Variation: Dialects, Sociolects, and Language Change
learning_objectives: Analyze language variation as systematic, not random or erroneous
Identify sociolinguistic variables: how speech varies by region, class, ethnicity, gender, and context
Understand that all language change is variation that has spread through a community over time
readings: Labov, 'The Social Stratification of English in New York City' (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: No one speaks 'a language.' Everyone speaks a dialect — a variety shaped by region, social class, ethnicity, gender, and situation. Sociolinguistics studies this variation, revealing that language is inseparable from social identity and power.
key_concepts: Dialect vs. language: 'a language is a dialect with an army and a navy' — the distinction is political, not linguistic
Sociolinguistic variables: features that vary systematically by social group (e.g., r-dropping in New York English)
Code-switching: moving between varieties depending on social context — a skill, not a deficiency
Language attitudes: the social judgments attached to different ways of speaking
Language change: all change begins as variation — yesterday's 'error' is tomorrow's standard
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a set of speech samples (transcriptions or recordings) to identify sociolinguistic variables and discuss what they reveal about the speakers' regional and social identities

review.key: A Language Is a Dialect with an Army
review.value: Max Weinreich's observation that the distinction between a language and a dialect is political, not linguistic — Cantonese and Mandarin are called 'dialects' of Chinese for political reasons, while Norwegian and Swedish are called separate 'languages' despite being mutually intelligible

The ability to move between language varieties depending on context — speaking one way with friends and another way in a job interview — is not confusion or inconsistency but a sophisticated sociolinguistic skill that demonstrates multilectal competence

questions.question: Transcribe the following English word using the IPA: 'through.' Then identify the phonemes and explain why English spelling does not reliably represent pronunciation.
questions.answer: IPA transcription: /θɹuː/. The word has three phonemes: the voiceless dental fricative /θ/, the alveolar approximant /ɹ/, and the long high back rounded vowel /uː/. English spelling does not reliably represent pronunciation because English orthography preserves historical spellings (the 'gh' in 'through' was once pronounced as a velar fricative) and because English has more phonemes than its 26-letter alphabet can represent.

questions.question: What is the difference between a phoneme and an allophone? Provide an English example.
questions.answer: A phoneme is a sound that distinguishes meaning in a language; an allophone is a predictable variant of a phoneme that does not change meaning. In English, /p/ is a phoneme. The aspirated [pʰ] (as in 'pat') and the unaspirated [p] (as in 'spat') are allophones of /p/ — they sound different physically but English speakers perceive them as the same sound because substituting one for the other never changes a word's meaning.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of phoneme and allophone
Explains why the distinction matters for meaning

questions.question: Break the word 'unbelievable' into its component morphemes. Identify each morpheme as free or bound, and as derivational or inflectional.
questions.answer: 'Unbelievable' contains three morphemes: (1) un- (bound, derivational prefix meaning 'not'), (2) believe (free, root morpheme), (3) -able (bound, derivational suffix meaning 'capable of being'). None of the morphemes are inflectional; all are derivational. The derivations change the root 'believe' (verb) to 'believable' (adjective) to 'unbelievable' (adjective with negative meaning).

rubric.key: Accurately classifies each as free or bound

questions.question: Draw phrase structure trees for the two possible interpretations of the ambiguous sentence: 'I saw the man with the telescope.'
questions.answer: Interpretation 1: I used the telescope to see the man. The PP 'with the telescope' attaches to the VP (modifying how I saw). Interpretation 2: I saw a man who had a telescope. The PP 'with the telescope' attaches to the NP 'the man' (modifying which man). The ambiguity is structural: the same string of words has two possible hierarchical structures, each producing a different meaning.

rubric.key: Notes that the ambiguity is structural, not lexical

major_assignments.title: Linguistic Analysis Project
major_assignments.description: Conduct a small-scale linguistic analysis of a language phenomenon of your choice. Options include: a phonological analysis of a dialect, a morphological analysis of a word formation process, a syntactic analysis of a construction, or a sociolinguistic analysis of language variation in a specific community. The project must use real linguistic data and apply the analytical methods studied in the course.
objectives: Apply linguistic analysis methods to real language data
Collect, organize, and analyze linguistic data systematically
Present findings using appropriate linguistic notation and terminology
Connect the analysis to broader linguistic principles
deliverables: Data appendix with organized linguistic examples
Linguistic analysis using appropriate notation (IPA, trees, morphological analysis)

criteria: Data is authentic and systematically collected
Data set is large enough to support the analysis
Data is organized clearly in the appendix
Linguistic methods are applied correctly and consistently
Analysis reveals genuine patterns in the data
Findings are connected to broader linguistic principles
Report is clearly written with appropriate terminology
Analysis is well-organized and easy to follow
Systematic analysis of authentic linguistic data
Rigorous application of linguistic methods and notation
Effective presentation connecting findings to linguistic principles

linguistic_analysis_project.description: Small-scale linguistic analysis of a language phenomenon

problem_sets.description: Weekly problem sets applying phonological, morphological, and syntactic analysis

quizzes.description: Quizzes on linguistic terminology and analytical methods

participation.description: Class participation and in-class analysis exercises

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all subfields of linguistics studied

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory. Linguistics is a cumulative subject; missing a class creates gaps that compound. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a letter grade deduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Problem sets penalized 10% per day late. The analysis project deadline is firm.
course_policies.collaboration: Problem sets may be discussed with classmates but must be written individually. The analysis project is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be your own. Data fabrication is a serious academic offense.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to IPA fonts and linguistic notation tools (available free online), word processing software, and LMS access.
